justification: 4 bounded contexts clearly identified and named (Route Planning, Delivery Management, Fleet & Compliance, Customer Notification) — exceeds the 3 minimum. Cross-context dependencies are visible through rule organization (R7/R8 span Fleet & Route Planning, R2/R3 triggers cross Delivery→Route boundaries, E2 explicitly shows cross-context vehicle class filtering). Vehicle class constraint correctly marked invariant: true (R7). Stop time correctly marked invariant: false/soft (R5). Route recalculation triggers correctly enumerated as 3 events (R2-R4). 6 formal Given/When/Then acceptance criteria plus 10 edge case scenarios far exceed the 4 minimum. Minor miss: notification window (R9) marked as invariant: true when the '3 stops' threshold is a configurable policy, and cross-context dependencies are not surfaced as a formal integration point section for MJ — they're distributed across rules rather than called out in a dedicated analysis.

observations.text: 4 well-defined bounded contexts with clear Owns/Does-NOT-Own boundaries — strongest context separation seen
12 business rules with correct invariant classification for vehicle class (true) and stop time (false)
Ubiquitous language glossary with 11 precisely defined terms — excellent domain modeling
6 acceptance criteria + 10 edge cases — comprehensive coverage including recalculation storms, multi-driver zones, and shift-end behavior
Route recalculation triggers correctly identified as 3 event-driven rules (new delivery, failed delivery, driver deviation) with invariant: true
Critical questions section surfaces 6 unresolved domain ambiguities that would derail implementation
Confidence assessment with explicit assumptions and low-confidence areas shows intellectual honesty
Next-3-stops notification window (R9) marked invariant: true, but the '3' threshold is a configurable policy parameter, not a hard invariant
Cross-context dependencies not surfaced as formal integration points for MJ — no dedicated section identifying data contracts or event flows between contexts

No explicit mention that route recalculation is event-driven across context boundaries as a distinct architectural concern — the triggers are enumerated but the cross-boundary event flow isn't called out as an integration design problem

agent_output_excerpt:
# Domain Analysis — Smart Route Optimization

## Domain Analysis

### Business Context

This feature encodes the **dynamic dispatch and route optimization process** for a last-mile delivery platform. It sits at the intersection of three core business processes: **shift management** (driver availability), **delivery fulfillment** (package lifecycle), and **fleet compliance** (vehicle-delivery matching). The "smart" aspect is the continuous recalculation — this is not a plan-once-and-execute model
